entries.title: IANA Time Zone Database

assessments.notes: Records post-1970 civil-time transitions for representative tzdb zones.
Does not provide authoritative legal polygons or administrative boundary assignments.
Contains some pre-1970 material, but its own theory file warns that complete past-time handling everywhere is out of scope.

caveats: Use for offset-rule history, not as complete legal-boundary evidence.

entries.title: Unicode CLDR time-zone metadata

assessments.notes: Provides localized time-zone names, aliases, and interoperability metadata.
Not a legal or geospatial boundary authority.

caveats: Useful for labels and mappings, not for scoring legal zone boundaries.

entries.title: National statutes, regulations, and official maps

assessments.notes: Best authority for current legal claims when current and accessible.
May define legal boundaries textually or by map, but availability and machine readability vary.
Historical law can be authoritative, but coverage, language, and amendment history vary by jurisdiction.

caveats: Requires country-specific audit for licensing, language, currency, and historical completeness.

entries.title: Official administrative boundary geometry

assessments.notes: Primary input for state, county, province, municipality, or district graph construction.
Supports legal time-zone scoring only when time law aligns to these units or assignments are independently derived.
Boundary geometry alone does not identify the legal UTC offset.

caveats: Must be paired with legal time-zone assignments and source vintages.

entries.title: Population weights and representative points

assessments.notes: Needed for population-weighted error and disruption metrics.
Needed to compute longitude-derived solar offset when geometry is not directly integrated.
Representative points and population tables do not establish legal time-zone boundaries.

caveats: Centroid, internal-point, and population-center choices can change measured solar error.
